---
title: "State Control of Media Influences Large Language Models"
subtitle: "Interactive companion to the research paper and replication with current models"
toc: false
---

**Hannah Waight**^1,2^, **Eddie Yang**^1,3^, **Yin Yuan**^4^, **Solomon Messing**^5^, **Margaret E. Roberts**^4^, **Brandon M. Stewart**^6^, **Joshua A. Tucker**^5,7^

::: {style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #666; margin-bottom: 1.5rem;"}
^1^Co-first author · ^2^University of Oregon · ^3^Purdue University · ^4^UC San Diego · ^5^NYU Center for Social Media, AI, and Politics · ^6^Princeton University · ^7^NYU Wilf Family Department of Politics
:::

[Paper (coming soon)]{.btn .btn-outline-secondary .btn-sm .me-1}
[GitHub](https://github.com/solomonmessing/llm_propaganda_web){.btn .btn-outline-secondary .btn-sm .me-1}

**Interactives:**<br/>
[Training Data](contamination.qmd){.btn .btn-primary .btn-sm .me-1}
[Live Audit](audit.qmd){.btn .btn-primary .btn-sm .me-1}
[Live Mem. Audit](memorization.qmd){.btn .btn-primary .btn-sm .me-1}
[Checkpoints](checkpoints.qmd){.btn .btn-primary .btn-sm .me-1}
[Live Crossnational Audit](global.qmd){.btn .btn-primary .btn-sm .me-1}

---

## Abstract

Millions of people around the world query large language models for information. While several studies have compellingly documented the persuasive potential of these models, there is limited evidence of who or what influences the models themselves, leading to a flurry of concerns about which companies and governments build and regulate the models. We show through six studies that government control of the media *already* influences the output of large language models via their training data. To understand the specific mechanism of how government control can influence LLMs, we begin with a case study of China's media. We demonstrate that media scripted and coordinated by the Chinese state appears in large language model training datasets. To evaluate the plausible effect of this inclusion, we use an open-weight model to show that additional pretraining on Chinese state-coordinated media generates more positive answers to prompts about Chinese political institutions and leaders. We link this phenomenon to commercial models through two audit studies demonstrating that prompting models in Chinese generates more positive responses about China's institutions and leaders than the same queries in English. China's media system is just one specific case of government control. We use a cross-national audit to provide evidence that the influence of media control on LLM outputs extends beyond China. We show that the languages of countries with lower media freedom exhibit a stronger pro-regime valence than those with higher media freedom. The combination of influence and persuasive potential suggests the troubling conclusion that states and powerful institutions have increased strategic incentives to leverage media control in the hopes of shaping large language model output.

---

## Logical Flow of the Six Studies

The six studies form a chain of evidence, starting with the presence of state media in open training corpora, moving through pretraining experiments and commercial model audits, and culminating in a cross-national analysis. Green boxes denote studies that rely on open data or model weights; purple boxes denote studies of commercial systems.

![Logical flow of the six studies.](images/figure_1_logical_flow.svg)

---

## State-Controlled Media in Training Data

### Study 1 — State Media in Training Data

A 5-word-gram similarity analysis of CulturaX finds that 3.1 million Chinese-language documents (1.64%) match state coordinated media corpora — a rate roughly 41 times that of Chinese Wikipedia. For documents that mention political leaders or institutions, match rates climb as high as 24%. [Explore state media in training data →](contamination.qmd)

![Match rates between CulturaX training data and Chinese state coordinated media sources.](images/fig2_panel_a_match.png){width=50%}

### Study 2 — Models Have Memorized State Media Content

When prompted with the first half of distinctive state media phrases, commercial models reproduce the expected continuation 3–10% of the time — matching or exceeding the rate at which they complete general web text from CulturaX. [Explore memorization evidence →](memorization.qmd)

![Memorization rates of state media phrases vs. general web phrases across commercial LLMs.](images/fig2_panel_b_mem.png){width=50%}

---

## State Media Exposure Shifts Model Output

### Study 3 — Pretraining on State Media

Fine-tuning Llama-2-13b on state coordinated media shifts its outputs toward greater favorability. With as few as 6,400 training documents, the fine-tuned model generates a more pro-government response nearly 80% of the time relative to the base model. This effect also transfers across languages, most strongly to traditional Chinese, Japanese, and to a lesser extent Korean — languages whose writing systems share tokens with simplified Chinese. [Explore pretraining results →](checkpoints.qmd)

::: {layout-ncol=2}
![Favorability increases with state coordinated media pretraining.](images/ptrain_main.png)

![Cross-lingual spillover effects.](images/ptrain_spillover.png)
:::

---

## Influence in Commercial LLMs

### Studies 4 & 5 — Audit of Production Models

Querying commercial models in Chinese rather than English yields systematically more positive portrayals of Chinese political leaders and institutions. In a blinded evaluation, nine annotators rated the Chinese-language response as more favorable in 75.3% of comparisons. The pattern replicates with naturalistic prompts drawn from WildChat and Chinese Q&A platforms, scales with model size, and extends to prompts about Russia and North Korea. [Try the model audit →](audit.qmd)

![LLM-judge audit showing cross-lingual favorability differences across production models.](images/fig4_panel_b_audit.png){width=50%}

::: {layout-ncol=2}
![Human annotation results.](images/fig4_panel_a_human.png)

![WildChat real user prompt analysis.](images/fig4_panel_c_wildchat.png)
:::

---

## Media Freedom Predicts LLM Favorability Worldwide

### Study 6 — Cross-National Generalization

Across 37 language-exclusive countries, LLMs prompted in the local language produce more regime-favorable responses where media freedom is low. Countries at the high end of the press-freedom spectrum show little difference from the English baseline, and in some cases a slight negative association. The pattern points to a relationship between state media environments and LLM outputs that generalizes beyond the China case.

![Cross-national analysis of LLM favorability and media freedom across 37 countries.](images/global_country_grid.png){width=100%}
